subtopics.subtopic_title: ONLINE GROOMING SEXTORTION AND TECHNOLOGY FACILITATED SEXUAL EXPLOITATION
subtopics.category: COMMERCIAL DIGITAL AND LABOR EXPLOITATION OF CHILDREN
subtopics.concept:
- **Core concept:** Online grooming, sextortion, and technology-facilitated sexual exploitation may serve as either the primary abuse or a gateway to offline assault. Grooming typically progresses through flattery, emotional dependency, and gradual sexualization rather than overt violence. Warning signs include sudden secrecy about devices, unexplained gifts or money, possession of explicit images, fear after online activity, or threats that images will be shared. Victims often present with anxiety, school avoidance, self-harm, or sleep disturbances, even in the absence of physical findings.
- **Clinical application:** Clinicians should document the platform used, the age or identity claimed by the perpetrator, and whether the child was asked to produce images or meet in person. Secure screenshots or photographs of chats, threats, or payment requests if this can be done safely and lawfully, but do not delete material that may have evidentiary value. Immediate actions include separating the child from the perpetrator’s digital access, involving safe caregivers, and notifying police or the National Cyber Crime Reporting Portal (https://cybercrime.gov.in/Webform/FAQ.aspx) alongside child protection referrals. Assessment should also include evaluation for coexisting risks like blackmail, trafficking, coercive sexual contact, pregnancy, sexually transmitted infections, self-harm, and threats to siblings.
- **Safety/follow-up:** Follow-up mental health care is essential, as shame and fear of image circulation can persist long after the online contact ends. Engage caregivers who are safe to provide emotional support and monitor the child’s recovery. Clinicians should ensure coordination with child protection services and law enforcement to address ongoing risks. Awareness of legal frameworks like the Protection of Children from Sexual Offences Act, 2012 (https://www.indiacode.nic.in/bitstream/123456789/2079/1/AA2012-32.pdf) is critical for appropriate escalation and safeguarding.

subtopics.subtopic_title: DOCUMENTATION PHOTOGRAPHY CHAIN OF CUSTODY AND VERBATIM DISCLOSURE RECORDING
subtopics.category: DOCUMENTATION REPORTING AND CHILD PROTECTION PATHWAYS IN INDIA
subtopics.concept:
- **Core concept:** Good documentation is both a clinical and legal intervention, forming a critical part of child protection. Essential elements include recording who brought the child, the date and time of examination, the child’s exact words in quotation marks, and the caregiver’s explanation separately. Findings should be described factually, detailing size, shape, color, tenderness, depth, location, and whether lesions are patterned or healing, without interpretive or speculative language.
- **Clinical application:** Use body diagrams and dated photographs with a scale to document findings, ensuring the person taking the photographs and the labeling process are recorded. For forensic samples like swabs, blood, or clothing, maintain a clear chain of custody, noting time, handler, and transfer destination. Document all clinical actions, including pain relief, wound care, counseling, specimen preservation, consultations, and the safety disposition. In neglect cases, record barriers explored, assistance offered, and whether the caregiver understood the plan; in fabricated illness, distinguish observed events from caregiver-reported ones.
- **Safety/follow-up:** High-quality documentation reduces the need for the child to repeat disclosures, minimizing trauma and improving communication within the care team. It often becomes the most durable evidence in legal proceedings, which may occur weeks or months later. Avoid speculative or judgmental language, focusing instead on why findings are concerning and documenting considered differentials. Clear and precise documentation strengthens child protection efforts and supports adherence to legal frameworks like the Protection of Children from Sexual Offences Act, 2012, and the Juvenile Justice (Care and Protection of Children) Act, 2015.

case_studies.title: Documentation of Suspected Child Abuse in a 7-Year-Old
case_studies.vignette: A 7-year-old girl is brought to the emergency department by her neighbor, who reports concerns about repeated injuries and the child’s reluctance to return home. The neighbor states that the child disclosed, 'My uncle hits me when no one is watching.' On examination, the child appears withdrawn and avoids eye contact. Multiple bruises of varying stages of healing are noted on the upper arms and back. A distinct, linear bruise measuring approximately 5 cm in length is seen on the right forearm. The caregiver’s explanation for the injuries is unavailable, as no family member accompanies the child. Photographs of the injuries are taken with a calibrated scale, and the person taking the photographs notes the date, time, and their name. A body diagram is used to map the injuries, and the child’s statement is documented verbatim in quotation marks. The neighbor’s account is recorded separately. A forensic kit is prepared, and the chain of custody is initiated, with detailed records of the time and handlers of all collected samples. Counseling is provided to the child, and the safety disposition is discussed with social services.
case_studies.learning_objective: To understand the importance of precise documentation, including verbatim statements, photographic evidence, and chain of custody, in cases of suspected child abuse.

case_studies.title: Documentation in Suspected Child Abuse Case
case_studies.vignette: A 7-year-old girl is brought to the emergency department at 9:30 PM by her maternal aunt, who reports that the child 'fell down the stairs' earlier in the evening. On examination, the child appears withdrawn and avoids eye contact. Multiple bruises of varying sizes and colors are noted on her back and upper arms. A crescent-shaped bruise is observed on her left forearm, and a healing laceration approximately 2 cm long is present on her scalp. When asked privately, the child states, 'My uncle hit me with a stick because I didn’t finish my homework.' The aunt, when questioned separately, insists the injuries are from repeated falls due to the child being 'clumsy.' A body diagram is used to mark the location and size of each injury, and photographs are taken with a scale for reference. The staff member taking the photographs documents their name, the date, and the time. The child’s statement, the caregiver’s explanation, and the findings are recorded verbatim and factually in the medical record. The chain of custody for the clothing, which has blood stains, is initiated for forensic evaluation.
case_studies.learning_objective: Demonstrate the principles of accurate and detailed documentation, including verbatim disclosures, factual injury descriptions, photography protocols, and chain of custody in suspected child abuse cases.

subtopics.subtopic_title: LEGAL AND PROTECTIVE PATHWAYS FOR PHYSICAL ABUSE EMOTIONAL ABUSE AND NEGLECT
subtopics.category: DOCUMENTATION REPORTING AND CHILD PROTECTION PATHWAYS IN INDIA
subtopics.concept:
- **Core concept:** Physical abuse, emotional abuse, and neglect in children are addressed within the juvenile justice and child welfare framework in India. The Juvenile Justice (Care and Protection of Children) Act, 2015, classifies an abused or neglected child as a 'child in need of care and protection' and mandates their presentation before the Child Welfare Committee (CWC). Acute cases of assault may also necessitate police registration under general criminal law, but the child protection pathway remains critical even in the absence of immediate criminal charges.
- **Clinical application:** Pediatricians must adopt a dual approach: treating injuries and ensuring immediate safety while notifying appropriate child protection authorities. Documentation should clearly outline who assumes responsibility for the child post-intervention. Practical entry points for urgent cases include the District Child Protection Unit and Child Helpline 1098, which facilitate rescue, shelter, kinship care, or interagency coordination. The CWC may oversee parental restoration under monitoring, direct services, or temporary placement with a fit person or facility when the home environment is unsafe.
- **Safety/follow-up:** Children with severe injuries, abandonment, dangerous neglect, or credible ongoing threats must not be discharged without ensuring a safe disposition. Pediatricians bear both medical and legal responsibility to prevent further harm by activating protection systems before discharge. Inaction can result in the child being returned to an unsafe environment, underscoring the importance of timely and decisive intervention. For children in institutional care, on the streets, or in labor settings, police involvement may be required for cases involving trafficking or assault.

subtopics.subtopic_title: HOUSEHOLD CONTACT CHILD SCREENING AFTER AN INDEX ABUSE CASE
subtopics.category: DOCUMENTATION REPORTING AND CHILD PROTECTION PATHWAYS IN INDIA
subtopics.concept:
- **Core concept:** When a child is identified as a likely victim of abuse, all other children in the same household or caregiving environment, including siblings, cousins, foster children, or institutional roommates, must be evaluated for potential occult injury or neglect. Younger children, particularly those under 12 months, are at higher risk due to their limited ability to disclose abuse and the higher likelihood of hidden injuries. This approach shifts the focus from an individual injury to addressing household-level safety concerns, potentially preventing further harm.
- **Clinical application:** All household contact children should undergo a detailed history and physical examination, with attention to bruising, oral injury, growth, hygiene, supervision, and developmental status. Imaging is recommended for asymptomatic younger children, as physical exams alone may miss occult fractures or intracranial injuries. Infants under 12 months typically require skeletal surveys and brain imaging after specialist review, while children aged 12–24 months commonly undergo skeletal surveys. Selective imaging may be appropriate for children up to 60 months when concerns are significant, and older children require targeted investigations based on findings and exposure history.
- **Safety/follow-up:** Each contact child must be documented separately, and any new injuries discovered should trigger an independent child protection response. Growth monitoring, immunization status, and caregiving arrangements should be reviewed for all contact children, as neglect often reflects broader household issues rather than isolated cases. Comprehensive assessment of contact children helps to identify and mitigate risks, ensuring a safer environment for all vulnerable individuals in the household.

subtopics.subtopic_title: MINIMUM ESSENTIAL WORKUP WHEN SPECIALIST SERVICES ARE UNAVAILABLE
subtopics.category: RESOURCE ADAPTED EVALUATION AND SAFE TRANSFER IN LIMITED SETTINGS
subtopics.concept:
- **Core concept:** In settings where advanced imaging, forensic kits, or pediatric subspecialists are unavailable, the minimum essential workup focuses on harm reduction and early escalation. For suspected physical abuse, perform a full undressed examination, photograph visible injuries, document developmental ability, and obtain basic blood tests like complete blood count and coagulation profile for unexplained bruising or bleeding. For sexual abuse, gentle examination, preservation of clothing in paper bags, labeled swabs, and meticulous documentation remain critical even without specialist access.
- **Clinical application:** When skeletal surveys cannot be completed locally, obtain plain radiographs of obvious injury sites and prioritize referral rather than dismissing concerns. For serious head injuries where neuroimaging is unavailable, stabilize airway and circulation, document neurologic status, and arrange urgent transfer. Baseline samples for infection testing should be collected when possible, and urgent medical needs must be addressed before arranging follow-up or referral for definitive testing. Teleconsultation with higher centers can guide decisions on imaging, prophylaxis, and transfer.
- **Safety/follow-up:** Smartphone photographs are acceptable for documenting injuries when formal medicolegal cameras are unavailable, provided confidentiality and secure handling are maintained. The guiding principle in limited-resource settings is not perfection but harm reduction—perform essential examinations, preserve evidence, ensure child safety, and escalate early to avoid missed diagnoses. Follow-up or referral should be arranged promptly for definitive testing, advanced care, or specialist evaluation as needed.

subtopics.subtopic_title: MULTIDISCIPLINARY FOLLOW UP FOR PHYSICAL SEXUAL AND PSYCHOLOGICAL RECOVERY
subtopics.category: MULTIDISCIPLINARY FOLLOW UP TRAUMA INFORMED CARE AND PREVENTION
subtopics.concept:
- **Core concept:** Multidisciplinary follow-up for children recovering from abuse must address physical recovery, psychological recovery, and the durability of the safety plan. This includes scheduled re-examinations for conditions like fractures, burns, abusive head trauma, or malnutrition, with repeat skeletal surveys or delayed infection testing as indicated. Survivors of sexual assault require medication reviews, pregnancy testing when relevant, repeat infection screenings, and ongoing assessment for pain, bleeding, sleep disturbances, and trauma-related symptoms. Mental health care, including trauma-focused therapy and caregiver support for the non-offending parent, is essential to minimize long-term impairment.
- **Clinical application:** Anticipatory guidance during follow-up should emphasize non-violent discipline, age-appropriate supervision, safe bathing and sleep practices, online safety, and fostering respect for bodily autonomy. Children should be encouraged to disclose unsafe touch or frightening experiences to trusted adults. For families under strain, early referrals to resources like nutrition support, disability services, domestic violence programs, substance-use treatment, and parenting assistance can mitigate risks before further harm occurs. Coordination with schools and community agencies is crucial to ensure comprehensive care and support.
- **Safety/follow-up:** Reliable documentation, active hospital child protection teams, and staff education are critical to improving outcomes for both current and future cases of child maltreatment. Linkage with community agencies enhances the safety net for vulnerable children and families. Clinicians must remain vigilant for subtle or easily missed signs of abuse and ensure that follow-up plans are robust and individualized. Prevention efforts should be integrated into routine pediatric practice to address systemic risks and promote long-term safety.

case_studies.title: Multidisciplinary Follow-Up for a 10-Year-Old Survivor of Abuse
case_studies.vignette:
A 10-year-old girl from a low-income urban neighborhood in India is brought to the outpatient clinic for follow-up, two weeks after being discharged from the hospital. She had presented initially with multiple bruises, a forearm fracture, and vaginal bleeding, which were determined to result from physical and sexual abuse by a neighbor. At discharge, she was placed in the temporary custody of her maternal aunt, as her mother was undergoing treatment for substance use. 

During today’s visit, the girl complains of persistent wrist pain despite wearing a cast and reports difficulty sleeping due to recurring nightmares. She appears withdrawn and avoids eye contact. On examination, her cast is intact, but there is mild swelling around the fingers. No new injuries are noted. Her weight is in the 10th percentile for age. A urine pregnancy test and repeat STI screening are pending as per the discharge plan. The aunt expresses concern about the child's refusal to attend school and frequent episodes of crying. 

The clinician reviews the safety plan and discusses the importance of trauma-focused therapy. Coordination with child protection services, school counselors, and a community nutrition program is planned.
case_studies.learning_objective: Understand the role of multidisciplinary follow-up in addressing the physical, psychological, and social recovery of children who have experienced abuse, including ongoing safety assessments and caregiver support.

subtopics.subtopic_title: TRAUMA INFORMED CARE RESILIENCE BUILDING AND PRIMARY PREVENTION STRATEGIES
subtopics.category: MULTIDISCIPLINARY FOLLOW UP TRAUMA INFORMED CARE AND PREVENTION
subtopics.concept:
- **Core concept:** Trauma-informed care emphasizes both the content and delivery of communication with abused children. Neutral procedures may be misinterpreted as threats, so clinicians should explain each step in simple language, revisit consent and assent, and provide as much safe control to the child as possible. A calm tone, predictable sequencing, and validating statements like 'this was not your fault' are essential to reduce retraumatization. Resilience is defined as the recovery capacity built through protective relationships and structured support, not merely the absence of harm.
- **Clinical application:** Clinicians should encourage the presence of a trusted, non-offending caregiver during evaluations when safe, as this can improve emotional regulation and disclosure. Older children benefit from practical coping strategies such as paced breathing, identifying safe adults, and creating actionable plans for safety if the perpetrator reapproaches. Identifying and leveraging strengths like supportive teachers, grandparents, or school counselors is critical, and these supports should be actively integrated into care plans. Families should be connected to services that alleviate stress, such as food support, disability assistance, domestic violence resources, and parenting education.
- **Safety/follow-up:** Discuss adverse childhood experiences (ACEs) with caregivers in a non-accusatory manner to foster understanding of the importance of early therapy and environmental changes. Trauma-informed care reduces distress during clinical visits while promoting the stable, nurturing environments necessary for long-term healing and normal development. Follow-up should focus on linking families to multidisciplinary resources to prevent recurrent stress and ensure ongoing support. Clinicians should remain vigilant for signs of retraumatization or unmet needs, escalating care as needed to specialized services.

subtopics.subtopic_title: BRUISED INFANT WITH SENTINEL INJURIES AND OCCULT TRAUMA
subtopics.category: DECISION PATHWAYS IN COMMON CHILD MALTREATMENT PRESENTATIONS
subtopics.concept:
- **Core concept:** Bruising in a non-ambulatory infant is a sentinel injury until proven otherwise, as minor external marks may coexist with serious occult trauma like rib fractures or intracranial bleeding. In the case of a 4-month-old with a lip bruise, torso bruises, and torn frenulum, the history of rolling from a couch should not be immediately reassuring. Sentinel injuries demand a high index of suspicion for child maltreatment, especially when the child is non-mobile. The goal is to exclude occult injury and ensure child safety through thorough evaluation and documentation.
- **Clinical application:** Management begins with admission and a comprehensive evaluation, including a full undressed examination, detailed oral inspection, coagulation studies, skeletal survey, and age-appropriate head imaging. Separate caregiver interviews and precise documentation of the timeline are critical, as histories often evolve during the evaluation. A normal radiograph or stable behavior in the emergency setting does not conclude the workup; a follow-up skeletal survey in approximately two weeks may reveal healing fractures that were initially missed. Ongoing observation is crucial to monitor for feeding difficulties, irritability, or neurologic changes that may emerge later.
- **Safety/follow-up:** The major pitfall is treating the case as a routine fall due to the calm demeanor of caregivers or the seemingly trivial nature of the injuries. Social work and child protection services must be notified early, even before definitive proof of abuse, to prevent further harm. The case should only be closed after occult injuries have been excluded and a documented safety plan ensures the child will not return unsupervised to a potentially abusive environment. Early intervention within the first 24 hours is critical to prevent a subsequent, potentially catastrophic assault.

case_studies.title: Bruised Infant with Sentinel Injuries
case_studies.vignette: A 4-month-old male infant is brought to the emergency department by his parents, who report that he 'rolled off the couch' earlier in the day. The infant is non-ambulatory and appears calm but has a faint bruise on his left cheek, a torn upper labial frenulum, and small bruises on his chest and abdomen. The parents state he cried briefly but has since been feeding normally. On examination, the infant is afebrile and alert but has mild irritability when handled. No signs of external head trauma or limb deformities are noted, and his anterior fontanelle is soft. The parents appear relaxed and insist the injury was accidental. No other injuries are visible on cursory inspection. A focused oral exam reveals a small laceration on the frenulum, and a full undressed examination shows faint bruising in non-bony areas. The caregivers provide inconsistent details about the timeline of the fall and the infant’s behavior afterward. Initial plain radiographs of the chest and abdomen are unremarkable. The infant remains stable during observation, but the bruising pattern raises concern for potential occult trauma.
case_studies.learning_objective: Recognize sentinel injuries in a non-ambulatory infant as potential indicators of child abuse and identify the need for thorough evaluation and safety planning to prevent further harm.

subtopics.subtopic_title: ADOLESCENT SEXUAL ASSAULT WITH EARLY HOSPITAL PRESENTATION
subtopics.category: DECISION PATHWAYS IN COMMON CHILD MALTREATMENT PRESENTATIONS
subtopics.concept:
- **Core concept:** Adolescent sexual assault requires immediate, sensitive, and legally compliant care. Confidentiality and its legal limits must be explained before examination, with private history-taking to clarify penetration type, condom use, ejaculation, strangulation risk, intoxication, and threats. A normal genital examination does not exclude assault; forensic sample collection should proceed based on timing and exposure history. In India, reporting is mandatory under the POCSO Act, but treatment, evidence collection, and safety planning must take precedence.
- **Clinical application:** Time-sensitive prophylaxis is critical in early hospital presentations. Presumptive antibiotics, emergency contraception (if conception is possible), hepatitis B review, and HIV post-exposure prophylaxis (PEP) eligibility assessment should be initiated promptly. Clothing preservation, photographs (if policy permits), and pregnancy testing are essential steps. Police paperwork or family conflict must not delay care, and clinicians should ensure adherence by providing clear written follow-up instructions for medication review, repeat testing, and psychological support.
- **Safety/follow-up:** Before discharge, confirm the adolescent’s immediate safety, including where she will sleep, whether the alleged perpetrator can access her, and whether a trusted adult can support medication adherence and follow-up visits. Adherence to HIV PEP often drops post-discharge, making follow-up crucial for medication completion and psychological recovery. Reporting under the POCSO Act is mandatory, but clinicians must prioritize timely care and evidence collection over procedural delays.

case_studies.title: Adolescent Sexual Assault with Early Presentation
case_studies.vignette:
A 15-year-old girl is brought to the emergency department by her 38-year-old mother within 6 hours of an alleged sexual assault. The incident occurred in her neighborhood while she was returning home after school. The adolescent appears withdrawn and avoids eye contact. On private history-taking, she discloses non-consensual vaginal penetration by an adult male neighbor. She reports that the perpetrator did not use a condom and ejaculated. There was no intoxication or strangulation involved. She denies any significant pain or bleeding but feels 'dirty' and is tearful. 

On examination, the patient is hemodynamically stable. No visible injuries are noted on general physical examination or external genitalia inspection. A urine pregnancy test is negative. The mother is visibly distressed and asks if the girl can avoid police involvement. 

The family resides in a crowded urban slum, and the patient is hesitant to return home, citing fear of further harm as the perpetrator lives nearby. She is unsure if she can adhere to any prescribed medications without assistance.

subtopics.subtopic_title: CHRONIC NEGLECT WITH FAILURE TO THRIVE AND MISSED CARE
subtopics.category: DECISION PATHWAYS IN COMMON CHILD MALTREATMENT PRESENTATIONS
subtopics.concept:
- **Core concept:** Chronic neglect with failure to thrive involves severe underweight, chronic diarrhea, incomplete immunization, and repeated missed clinic visits. Differentiating poverty from neglect requires assessing whether harm persists despite support. Objective documentation of growth charts, immunization gaps, and medical issues is essential to establish neglect as a protection concern.
- **Clinical application:** Management includes immediate medical stabilization with nutrition and hydration assessment, anemia or infection treatment, and confirmation of immunization gaps. Direct questioning about feeding, supervision, and clinic transportation is critical. Families should be linked to local resources like Anganwadi services, with concrete plans explained rather than vague advice. Follow-up evaluates caregiver adherence to the plan and the child’s progress.
- **Safety/follow-up:** Escalation is warranted when weight gain remains absent, medicines are not obtained, or unsafe supervision persists despite practical assistance. Protective referral becomes medically justified if growth, disease control, or safety remains compromised after realistic support. Avoid blaming poverty without evidence or dismissing harm due to financial stress.

case_studies.vignette: A 2-year-old boy is brought to a rural health center by his grandmother due to chronic diarrhea and poor weight gain. His mother, a single parent, works long hours as a daily laborer. The grandmother reports that the child has been sickly for months, with frequent watery stools and low appetite. He has not received vaccinations since 6 months of age and has missed several follow-up visits due to transportation issues and lack of money. On examination, the child is lethargic, weighs 6.5 kg (<3rd percentile), and has visible wasting with thin extremities. His skin is dry, and his abdomen is distended. Growth records from the Anganwadi center show stagnant weight for the past 6 months. No acute signs of dehydration are noted, but pallor is evident. When questioned, the grandmother admits that meals often consist only of diluted rice water, and there is no consistent caregiver when she is away. The family has not accessed any nutritional support programs despite being eligible.
case_studies.learning_objective: Recognize chronic neglect with failure to thrive through growth stagnation, incomplete immunization, and missed care, and assess the threshold for protective referral when harm persists despite support.

subtopics.subtopic_title: REPEATED HOSPITALIZATIONS SUGGESTING FABRICATED ILLNESS
subtopics.category: DECISION PATHWAYS IN COMMON CHILD MALTREATMENT PRESENTATIONS
subtopics.concept:
- **Core concept:** Repeated hospitalizations with normal evaluations and symptoms described only by the caregiver, as in the case of a 3-year-old with reported seizures but normal neurologic studies, may suggest caregiver-fabricated illness rather than a rare disease. Recognizing this requires consolidating records across hospitals, building a detailed timeline of reported versus observed symptoms, and determining whether prior diagnoses were based on objective findings or caregiver narratives. A single coordinating physician is essential to prevent fragmented reassessments and iatrogenic harm.
- **Clinical application:** When symptoms resolve during supervised separation or in the caregiver's absence, the likelihood of caregiver-induced illness increases significantly. Documentation should focus on factual observations, such as who witnessed what, when it occurred, and how the child behaved under direct supervision. Premature confrontation of the caregiver or accusatory language can lead to absconding, complaint withdrawal, or further harm to the child. Instead, involve child protection services, nursing leadership, social work, and mental health teams while maintaining medically necessary observation.
- **Safety/follow-up:** Protective restriction of caregiver access may be necessary even before a definitive diagnosis is established, as the immediate priority is preventing further harm and unnecessary medicalization. The decision to restrict access should focus on whether continued caregiver contact poses a risk of preventable illness rather than the caregiver's motive. Safety planning should be coordinated with child protection agencies to ensure the child's well-being while evidence is systematically organized.

subtopics.subtopic_title: SCHOOL REFUSAL AND ANXIETY FROM EMOTIONAL ABUSE
subtopics.category: DECISION PATHWAYS IN COMMON CHILD MALTREATMENT PRESENTATIONS
subtopics.concept:
- **Core concept:** School refusal, crying spells, nightmares, and somatic symptoms like abdominal pain in children may signal emotional abuse, especially when physical examination is normal. Emotional abuse is diagnosed through observed patterns and their impact on the child, rather than physical evidence like bruises. Key indicators include caregiver behaviors such as humiliation, intimidation, or verbal aggression, as well as the child’s fearfulness, withdrawal, or declining school attendance.
- **Clinical application:** Clinicians should treat caregiver-child interactions during visits as clinical data and document exact caregiver statements. Assess the child’s sense of safety at home, inquire about exposure to domestic violence, and screen for anxiety, depression, and self-harm. Input from teachers or counselors can help confirm patterns like declining attendance or fearfulness. Avoid mislabeling the issue as behavioral or academic noncompliance, as the root cause may be chronic caregiver maltreatment.
- **Safety/follow-up:** If the child is being terrorized, threatened, or exposed to severe domestic violence, immediate referral to child protection services is warranted, even in the absence of physical injury. For cases where removal from the home is not required, a structured plan involving trauma-focused therapy, school support, and close safety monitoring is essential. Early identification and intervention can prevent progression to self-harm, major depression, or physical assault.

case_studies.title: School Refusal and Anxiety in a 10-Year-Old Due to Emotional Abuse
case_studies.vignette: A 10-year-old girl is brought to a rural outpatient clinic in India by her mother due to persistent abdominal pain and refusal to attend school for the past two months. The mother describes her as 'lazy and stubborn,' frequently scolding her in front of the clinician. The child appears withdrawn, avoids eye contact, and clutches her stomach while sitting silently. On further questioning, she admits to frequent nightmares and crying spells at night, along with a fear of going to school. Her teacher has noted a significant decline in her academic performance and attendance. Physical examination, including abdominal palpation, is unremarkable, and her vital signs are stable. When asked about her home environment, the child hesitates but eventually mentions that her father often shouts and throws objects when angry. The mother dismisses this, saying, 'She just needs to toughen up.'
case_studies.learning_objective: Recognize school refusal, somatic complaints, and anxiety as potential indicators of emotional abuse, and assess caregiver-child dynamics and home safety to guide further intervention.

case_studies.title: School Refusal and Anxiety in a 9-Year-Old with Emotional Abuse
case_studies.vignette: A 9-year-old girl is brought to a community health center in a semi-urban area by her mother, complaining of frequent abdominal pain and reluctance to attend school for the past three months. Her mother describes her as 'lazy and overly sensitive,' claiming she cries for no reason and refuses to eat breakfast before school. The child avoids eye contact during the interview, appears withdrawn, and clutches her stomach when asked about her symptoms. She denies any recent fever, diarrhea, or vomiting. On examination, her vitals are normal, and no physical abnormalities are noted. When gently asked about school, the child begins to cry and says she feels scared but does not elaborate. The mother interjects, calling her 'useless' and 'a burden.' The child’s teacher, contacted by phone, reports that her attendance has been erratic, and she has been unusually quiet and tearful in class. The teacher also mentions the child has spoken about frequent arguments at home and being called names. A review of the child’s history reveals no prior medical conditions or hospitalizations.
case_studies.learning_objective: Recognize emotional abuse as a potential underlying cause of school refusal and somatic complaints, and understand the importance of assessing caregiver-child interactions and the child’s sense of safety.
